# What Are the Differences between Traditional Alloys and High-Entropy Alloys?

## Introduction

Traditional alloys and [high-entropy alloys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy) (HEAs) are two types of materials used in various applications. Both of them are composed of metallic elements, yet there are several key differences between these alloys. In this article, we will explore the differences between traditional alloys and HEAs, including their composition, microstructures, properties, applications, and production methods.

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1687655408-normal-automatic-model-of-heas.jpg)[1]

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1687655408-normal-automatic-model-of-heas.jpg)[1]

**Figure 1. Automatic Model of HEAs**

## Introduction to Traditional Alloys & High-entropy Alloys

[Traditional alloys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alloy#History_and_examples) combine one metal element with other metal or non-metal elements. These types of alloys reserve some properties of the original base metal while changing some features, such as enhanced strength or promoted ductility.

There are a wide variety of alloying elements that can be added to the base metals. Chromium is commonly added to enhance the corrosion resistant of the alloys; tungsten is a metal alloying element used to improve wear resistance at high temperatures; while carbon addition is frequently added in steel and cast iron alloys to improve strength.

High-entropy alloys refer to alloys containing 5 or more principal elements, each of which has an atomic percentage between 5% and 35%. The inclusion of multiple elements results in a complicated structure and high entropy effect, which bring about HEA’s unique physical and mechanical properties compared with conventional alloys.

## Traditional Alloys VS. High-entropy Alloys

### --Composition

Traditional alloys and high-entropy alloys have different element compositions. For one thing, conventional alloys are typically composed of two or three metallic elements in varying proportions. Whereas, HEAs contain at least five elements in equal or approximately equal atomic proportions. For another, the base metal in traditional alloys accounts for the largest proportion, while the elements in high-entropy alloys take up equal or near-equal proportions.

### --Microstructure

Different composition results in different structures. Traditional alloys typically exhibit a well-defined microstructure, such as a eutectic or dendritic structure, while HEAs exhibit a disordered, homogeneous microstructure. The disordered nature of HEAs leads to a random distribution of atoms, which reduces the occurrence of phase transformations, even at high temperatures.

### --Properties

Traditional alloys exhibit a range of properties, depending on their composition and microstructure. For example, some traditional alloys may be strong but brittle, while others may be ductile but have low strength.

In contrast, HEAs show a superior combination of properties, including high strength, ductility, and thermal stability, due to their disordered microstructure. For instance, VNbMoTaW is a remarkable refractory alloy with a high yield strength of over 600 MPa (87 ksi)) even at a temperature of 1,400 °C, which could even outperform conventional superalloys.

### --Applications

Traditional alloys are popular materials used in various applications, such as automotive, aerospace, and construction.

HEAs, on the other hand, are a relatively new class of materials that are still being explored for their potential applications. They have shown promise in various applications, including high-temperature thermoelectric applications, wear-resistant coatings, and structural materials.

For instance, HEAs are applied to the automobile industry to make engine parts, drivetrain components, and other structural equipment. These alloys also serve as promising materials for nuclear applications thanks to their high-temperature mechanical properties and radiation and corrosion resistance.

### --Production

The preparation process of conventional alloys is relatively simple, while there are multiple approaches to producing high-entropy alloys.

Traditional alloys are typically produced using conventional methods, such as casting, extrusion, and forging. Generally, the process includes melting, mixing, solidifying, and cooling thereafter.

Yet, the production of HEAs requires specialized techniques (see Figure 2) due to their complex composition and microstructure. Most of them are made with [arc melting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/arc-melting) and induction melting. Solid-state processing employs mechanical alloying. Gas-phase processing utilizes sputtering or molecular beam epitaxy (MBE).

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1687655430-normal-fabrication-of-hea.jpg)[2]

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1687655430-normal-fabrication-of-hea.jpg)[2]

**Figure 2. Fabrication of HEA**

## Conclusion

In a word, traditional alloys and high-entropy alloys differ in their composition, microstructure, properties, applications, and production methods. While traditional alloys have a well-defined microstructure and properties, HEAs offer a unique combination of properties due to their disordered, homogeneous microstructure. HEAs are a promising class of materials for various applications and are still being explored for their full potential. Highlights
----------

*   •The review addresses the high entropy as a transformative class of materials for the fourth industrial revolution materials. 
*   •Background studies from the stone age to the 4 th industrial revolution. 
*   •A comparative study was conducted on HEA, by highlighting its configuration, characteristics, and high entropy effects. 
*   •The advantages of High Entropy Alloys, particularly as a potential 4th Industrial Material, were reviewed. 
*   •Limitations and Challenges of High Entropy Alloys. 

Abstract
--------

High-entropy alloys (HEAs) have emerged as an innovative family of multi-principal element alloys with unique properties that position them as potential candidates for the fourth industrial revolution (Industry 4.0) materials. Characterized by their multi-principal element composition, HEAs exhibit exceptional mechanical strength, thermal stability, corrosion resistance, and tailored functional properties. They leverage high configurational entropy to deliver superior performance over traditional alloys such as steel, aluminum, and titanium. HEAs have demonstrated remarkable potential in critical sectors, including aerospace, automotive, energy, and biomedicine, with examples like NbMoTaW in jet engines and TiZrNbTaMo in medical implants showcasing their versatility under extreme conditions. However, challenges such as high processing costs, difficulties in large-scale production, limited understanding of phase stability, and the need for advanced computational models to predict material behavior must be addressed. This paper explores the prospects of HEAs as the fourth industrial material, discussing their advantages, potential applications, and the limitations that must be overcome to realize their full industrial potential. By integrating emerging manufacturing techniques such as additive manufacturing and computational material design, HEAs could revolutionize material engineering and contribute significantly to Industry 4.0. Transitioning from niche innovations to industrial mainstays, mirroring the defining impact of steel in the First Industrial Revolution and silicon in the Third, thereby cementing their place at the forefront of Industry 4.0’s high-performance and sustainable future.

1. Introduction
---------------

### 1.1. Background on industrial materials

Industrial materials have evolved remarkably, shaping the trajectory of human innovation and technological progress. In their earliest forms, traditional materials like wood, stone, and basic metals such as copper and iron defined the limits of engineering and construction [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib2)]. The Bronze Age marked a significant leap with the discovery of alloying, combining metals like copper and tin to create more substantial and durable materials [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib3)]. This was followed by the Iron Age, where iron and steel became cornerstones of industrial development due to their abundance and versatility. These foundational materials powered the first waves of industrialization, from rudimentary tools to the machinery of the First and Second Industrial Revolutions [[[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib4), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib6)].

As industries grew more complex, so did the demand for materials with enhanced properties. The 20th century ushered in modern alloy combinations like stainless steel, titanium alloys, and aluminum blends tailored for specific applications in aerospace, automotive, and manufacturing sectors [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib8)]. These alloys improved strength, corrosion resistance, and lightweight performance, aligning with the Third Industrial Revolution’s focus on precision, automation, and electronics [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib3)]. Alongside metals, synthetic polymers and ceramics emerged, broadening the material palette for specialized uses [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib9)].

Today, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, characterized by smart manufacturing, nanotechnology, and sustainability, drives the development of advanced materials [[[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib4), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib6)]. High-entropy alloys (HEAs) stand out as a promising candidate in this era. Unlike traditional alloys dominated by one principal element, HEAs blend multiple elements in near-equal proportions, yielding unique properties like exceptional strength, thermal stability, and resistance to wear [[[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12)]. First conceptualized in the early 2000s, HEAs represent a paradigm shift, leveraging entropy to stabilize complex structures that defy conventional metallurgy [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib13)]. They hold potential for cutting-edge applications, such as turbine blades, biomedical implants, and energy storage systems, aligning with the demands of Industry 4.0 for multifunctional, high-performance materials [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib14),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib15)]. The Ashby map shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#fig1) offers an insightful overview of material development spanning 10,000 years [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib16)]. It clearly illustrates the different categories of materials, such as ceramics, metals, polymers, and, more recently, composites. The map effectively showcases the progression from discovery to development and, ultimately, to the design of these materials.

Despite the advanced properties exhibited by HEAs, traditional alloys, including steels, titanium alloys, and aluminum-based materials, frequently demonstrate superior suitability for specific applications. This preference can be attributed to their cost-effectiveness, established processing methodologies, and proven long-term reliability. In cost-sensitive industries such as automotive manufacturing and construction, mild and low-alloy steels are particularly favored due to their low cost, ease of welding, and scalable production capabilities. Conversely, HEAs, which often incorporate costly elements such as Co, Ni, or Mo, and necessitate complex processing techniques such as vacuum arc melting or powder metallurgy, are generally considered economically unfeasible for large-scale applications [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib17)]. In the realm of biomedical applications, titanium alloys, notably Ti–6Al–4V, continue to dominate due to their established biocompatibility, corrosion resistance, and extensive clinical utility attributes that many HEAs have not yet consistently demonstrated or successfully navigated through regulatory scrutiny [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib18)]. Similarly, in cryogenic environments, such as liquefied natural gas storage and aerospace applications, traditional austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 304L and 316L) outshine many HEAs by maintaining exceptional ductility and fracture toughness at low temperatures. In contrast, HEAs tend to encounter challenges related to phase instability or embrittlement under such conditions [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib19)]. Regarding electrical and thermal conductivity, copper and aluminum alloys remain indispensable in electronics, motors, and heat exchangers. HEAs typically exhibit significantly lower conductivity, a consequence of severe lattice distortion and complex scattering phenomena [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib20)]. Furthermore, in high-performance aerospace engines, conventional nickel-based superalloys, such as Inconel 718, are still preferentially utilized due to their validated high-temperature creep resistance, oxidation protection, and established coating systems, attributes that HEAs have yet to replicate comprehensively under prolonged operational stress [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib21)]. Lastly, in manufacturing environments where formability, weldability, and machinability are paramount, traditional alloys benefit from decades of industrial optimization, robust supply chains, and predictable mechanical behavior. In contrast, many HEAs face obstacles related to high hardness, limited forming data, and a lack of standardized processing protocols [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib22)]. These considerations underscore that, while HEAs offer innovative property combinations in laboratory-scale investigations, traditional alloys remain essential in a wide array of engineering applications due to their cost efficiency, mature processing techniques, and verified long-term performance.

### 1.2. High entropy alloys

High entropy alloys represent an emergent class of materials that have captured the attention of scientists and engineers in recent decades. Emerging in the early 2000s, HEAs diverge from the traditional alloy design, which typically relies on one primary element (like Fe in steel or Al in its alloys) mixed with small amounts of others [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib10),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12),[[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib23), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib24), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib25)]. Instead, HEAs are defined as alloys composed of five or more principal elements, each present in near-equal atomic proportions, typically between 5% and 35% [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib26),[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib27)]. This multi-element approach maximizes configurational entropy, a thermodynamic property stabilizing their structure, giving rise to a single-phase solid solution rather than a brittle mix of compounds [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib25),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib28)]. First introduced by researchers like Jien-Wei Yeh and Brian Cantor, HEAs challenge conventional metallurgy and offer a new frontier for material innovation [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib22),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib29)].

The unique characteristics of HEAs stem from this high-entropy design. One standout feature is their exceptional mechanical strength, often retained across a wide range of temperatures [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib30)]. This arises from lattice distortions caused by the variety of atomic sizes, which impede dislocation movement, a key factor in deformation [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib31),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib32)]. HEAs also exhibit impressive corrosion resistance, as their complex chemistry can form protective oxide layers or resist degradation in harsh environments [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib33),[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib34)]. Thermal stability is another hallmark; many HEAs maintain their structure and properties at elevated temperatures where traditional alloys might soften or fail [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib35)]. Additional traits, like wear resistance and ductility, vary depending on composition, making HEAs highly tunable for specific needs [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib36),[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib37)].

In the context of "The Prospect and Limitation of High Entropy Alloy as 4th Industrial Material," HEAs hold immense promise for the Fourth Industrial Revolution’s demands. Their multifunctionality suits advanced applications, such as jet engine components enduring extreme heat, biomedical implants resisting bodily corrosion, or lightweight, durable parts for smart manufacturing systems [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12),[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib19),[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib38)]. The ability to tailor HEAs by adjusting their elemental mix aligns with Industry 4.0’s emphasis on customization, sustainability, and performance under complex conditions. For instance, alloys like CoCrFeNiMn have shown remarkable toughness at cryogenic temperatures, hinting at uses in space exploration or energy storage [[[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib39), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib40), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib41), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib42)].

However, HEAs face significant limitations. Their complex compositions can complicate manufacturing processes, increase costs, and pose challenges in scalability compared to established alloys like steel or titanium. Additionally, while their properties are impressive in lab settings, real-world performance under diverse conditions remains under scrutiny. The prospect of HEAs as a "4th Industrial Material" hinges on overcoming these hurdles, balancing innovation with practicality to integrate them into the smart, interconnected systems of the future. From humble stone tools to the promise of entropy-driven alloys, the journey of industrial materials reflects humanity’s relentless pursuit of progress, each step building on the last yet always reaching for what lies beyond.

### 1.3. Importance of HEAs in industry

High-entropy alloys are poised to play a pivotal role in modern industry, particularly as a potential "4th Industrial Material" aligning with the Fourth Industrial Revolution’s emphasis on smart, sustainable, and high-performance systems. Their superior properties position them as a versatile solution to meet the demands of cutting-edge technologies, from aerospace to biomedical engineering [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib30),[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib43),[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib44)]. As industries evolve toward automation, nanotechnology, and interconnected systems, HEAs offer a compelling case for driving innovation where traditional materials like steel, aluminum, or titanium alloys may fall short.

One of the key reasons HEAs are considered a game-changer is their mechanical prowess. Their high strength, often maintained across extreme temperatures, makes them ideal for applications under harsh conditions [[[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib45), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib46), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib47)]. For instance, in aerospace, where components like turbine blades must endure intense heat and stress, HEAs such as those based on refractory elements (e.g., NbMoTaW) outperform conventional superalloys in thermal stability [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib48),[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib49)]. This durability could lead to longer-lasting, more efficient engines, reducing maintenance costs and emissions, a priority in Industry 4.0’s sustainability focus. Similarly, their corrosion resistance shines in chemical processing or marine environments, where traditional metals degrade, offering a longer lifespan for critical infrastructure [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12),[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib50)].

The adaptability of HEAs further enhances their industrial potential. Unlike traditional alloys with fixed compositions, HEAs can be tailored by adjusting their elemental mix to optimize specific properties, such as lightweight for automotive parts or biocompatibility for medical implants [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib37)]. This flexibility aligns with the smart manufacturing ethos of Industry 4.0, where customization and rapid prototyping via techniques like 3D printing are paramount. A CoCrFeNi-based HEA can be optimized for toughness in cryogenic storage systems, aiding advancements in energy and space exploration. This multifunctionality reduces the need for multiple specialized materials, streamlining supply chains and boosting efficiency.

Moreover, HEAs’ unique microstructures stabilized by high configurational entropy enable properties that push beyond the limits of earlier industrial materials. Steel powered the First and Second Industrial Revolutions with strength and scalability; semiconductors defined the Third with electronic precision. With their blend of resilience and versatility, HEAs could underpin the Fourth by enabling technologies that integrate physical, digital, and biological systems. Imagine sensors or robotics components that resist wear and extreme conditions while remaining lightweight. HEAs could make this a reality, enhancing the reliability of interconnected, data-driven industrial ecosystems.

Their potential isn’t without context. The Fourth Industrial Revolution demands materials that support sustainability, and HEAs' durability could reduce waste by extending product lifecycles. Their use in high-efficiency turbines or battery components in energy sectors could advance renewable technologies [[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib51),[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib52)]. Yet, their industrial importance hinges on overcoming challenges like high production costs and scalability. If these hurdles are addressed, perhaps through innovations in manufacturing or computational design, HEAs could rival the revolutionary impact of steel or silicon, cementing their status as a defining material of Industry 4.0. Their superior properties offer a glimpse of a future where industrial limits are redefined, one alloy at a time.

### 1.4. Purpose of the review

The purpose of reviewing "The Prospect and Limitation of High Entropy Alloy as 4th Industrial Material" is to critically assess the potential of HEAs to redefine industrial applications while identifying the practical barriers that could impede their widespread adoption. This examination aims to bridge the gap between the theoretical promise of HEAs and their real-world viability, providing a balanced perspective for researchers, engineers, and industry leaders navigating the Fourth Industrial Revolution. By exploring their strengths and shortcomings, the review seeks to inform strategies for leveraging HEAs in cutting-edge technologies and guide future research and development efforts.

One primary goal is to evaluate the prospects of HEAs as a transformative material. This involves analyzing how their superior properties align with the demands of Industry 4.0, including smart manufacturing, sustainability, and advanced engineering. The review aims to highlight specific applications where HEAs could outperform traditional materials, such as aerospace components, energy systems, or biomedical devices, and assess their potential to drive innovation in these fields. Doing so establishes a clear case for why HEAs are being considered a candidate for the "4th Industrial Material," building on the legacy of steel, semiconductors, and other historical game-changers.

Equally important is the goal of identifying and understanding the limitations of HEAs. This includes examining challenges like complex manufacturing processes, high production costs, and scalability issues that could hinder their transition from laboratory success to industrial staples. The review aims to pinpoint gaps in current knowledge, such as long-term performance under real-world conditions, and assess whether these constraints outweigh the benefits in practical settings. By outlining these obstacles, the analysis provides a reality check against the enthusiasm surrounding HEAs, ensuring expectations remain grounded in feasibility.

HEA’S FOUR CORE EFFECTS Owing to each component’s equimolar concentration, HEA compositions are more complicated than those of ordinary alloys. HEAs have various distinct kinetic and thermodynamic features because of the revolutionary alloy design idea and high-concentration solid solution structure which enables the development of distinctive and functional properties [21]. According to the studies [11, 22, 23], the four core effects of HEAs can be summarized by the following aspects: (1) High-entropy processes known as hermodynamics allow a material to form simpler solid solution phases, frequently FCC and BCC. (2) The kinetics of microstructural level processes, including creep, grain development, and recrystallization are reduced by slow diffusion. (3) Severe lattice distortion enhances the chemical and mechanical characteristics of structures. (4) The interaction between the various elements improves the alloy’s characteristics in comparison to those that would be predicted from the law of mixes, which is how cocktail effects may be described on an atomic scale. The specifics of each consequence will be covered. The four main impacts and their connection to microstructure and characteristics are summarized in Figure 3 [14]. Figure 3. Four core effects and their relationships [14] 127 2.1 High entropy effect Basic structures like FCC and BCC have a lower Gibbs energy when all the components are present because there is a high number of unique elements in the mixture, which leads to a high mixing entropy. More solution phases arise in this way under conditions of high entropy. These high entropy phases are stabilized by this Gibbs energy reduction, leading to a simple microstructure [11]. According to the studies [24, 25], the high configurational entropy impact promotes the creation of solid solutions while impeding phase transformations that would otherwise affect the system’s thermodynamics [26]. Different binary to septenary alloys’ XRD patterns are displayed in Figure 4. The concept that both ternary and binary compounds would evolve defies such primary structural forms [27]. Figure 4. The XRD patterns of an alloy produced by sequentially adding an extra element to the one before [27] The only two essential phases of senary, septenary, and quinary alloys are BCC and FCC, which have simplified structures. This aspect indicates that these alloys’ phases remain pretty simple (note that the septenary alloy basically contains phases of minor intermetallic, although unclear in the XRD pattern). 2.2 Sluggish diffusion effect Sluggish diffusion affects the system’s kinetics; it positively affects thermal stability, recrystallization temperature, grain development, phase separation, and creep resistance, which may be advantageous to the microstructure [28]. The diffusion process may become slow, and the system’s temperature may drop if the number of elements in alloy increases [24, 29]. 2.3 Severe lattice distortion Because of the significant entropy influence, HEAs consist of a single matrix, whether an FCC, BCC, or other structure. As a result, each component joins the whole. The resulting solid solution’s lattice is severely deformed since each element has a unique atomic size, bonding energy with other elements, and inclination toward crystal formation. Lattice distortion affects the final properties of the alloys, such as their hardness and tensile strength, because it inhibits dislocations from freely migrating as expected [30, 31]. Another significant result is that the features’ dependence on temperature is reduced [11, 24]. Mismatch in shear modulus between the atoms’ constituents, which leads to hardening, and the electronic structures linked to variations in local bond states, for example, may have an impact on thermal and electrical conductivities [12, 32, 33]. 2.4 The ‘cocktail’ effect Figure 5. Hardness of the AlxCoCrCuFeNi alloys as a function of Al content [27] HEAs can be viewed as a composite on an atomic level. This feature confirms that interactions between different components produce properties other than those expected by the rule of mixtures. Additional impacts of the cocktail effect include hardening the system and encouraging the production of a particular phase, like aluminum, which can produce either FCC or BCC structures depending on its concentration [11, 34]. The “cocktail” effect takes into account the powerful structural and functional capabilities of “gum” metals as well as the remarkably distinctive qualities of totally amorphous bulk metallic glasses [12, 35, 36]. The “cocktail” effect serves as a warning to be open-minded to non-linear, unexpected consequences that may follow from odd assemblages of components and microstructures in the ample composition space of multi-principal element alloys MPEAs [12]. Figure 5 describes the hardness of the AlxCoCrCuFeNi alloy as a function of Al concentration. It is evident that adding Al could make the alloy significantly more challenging. Al’s larger atomic size, the emergence of a complex BCC phase, and more cohesive bonding with other elements are all contributing factors. Hence, the macroscopic characteristics of HEA include the averaged properties of the component elements and the effects of the surplus quantities caused by inter-elemental reactions and lattice distortion [27]. 3. HIGH ENTROPY ALLOY PROPERTIES Tying together composition, microstructure, and property data provides new challenges in the research of HEAs. The quantity of alloys which must be significantly defined grows due to the wide range of compositions. In light of the fact that components are usually more localized in HEAs than in traditional alloys, regardless of the same alloy family, changing just one element can considerably impact microstructure and characteristics. Accordingly, the following section will examine HEA’s physical (electrical and magnetic), mechanical, and thermal properties. 128 3.1 Electrical properties For the same composition, the FCC phase has a larger electrical resistivity than the BCC phase, and the linear average of the volume fractions of the BCC and FCC phases determines the electrical resistivity in a two-phase field. For instance, the usual range of electrical resistivity for AlxCoCrFeNi alloys (0 × 2) is 100-200 cm. The electrical resistance of each of these alloys rises with a linear trend with temperature. A non-monotonic electrical resistivity dependence could result from the microstructures switching from “FCC” to “BCC” as the Al content rises. Electrical resistivity is predicted to rely monotonically no longer on Al concentration with the switch from BCC to FCC microstructures. Cold-rolled Comparing homogenized materials to cold-rolled alloys, resistance is increased [37-39]. 3.2 Magnetic properties To prevent materials from being strained by an external magnetic field, the magnetostriction effect must be very modest. Relying on the number of current magnetic components, HEA’s magnetic characteristics could be modified. Processing and thermal history have an impact on magnetic characteristics through the phases that are created. Compared to annealed materials, as-processed materials frequently have unique microstructures and magnetic characteristics [12, 40]. Investigations on the magnetic characteristics of HEAs have mainly concentrated on alloys made of Cr, Co, Al, Cu, Ni, Ti, and Fe. Higher magnetization often represents the result of having more magnetic elements. However, adding components to alloys can have a considerable influence. For instance, magnetization is decreased when Cr is added. The conclusion reached by Zhang et al. [26] that the presence of Cr causes the cancellation of magnetization appears to be supported by the observation that the separation of Cr from Fe and Co results in increased magnetization. Like conventional magnetic materials, higher coercivities are associated with finer microstructures [41]. 3.3 Thermal properties Thermal conductivity, K (T), is frequently determined by measuring a material’s density, specific heat, and thermal diffusion coefficient (T). The region with the lowest thermal conductivity is the FCC + BCC duplex [22]. Low Al concentration single-phase FCC alloys have a thermal conductivity almost half that of high Al content (single-phase BCC alloys). As Al concentration rises in single-phase zones, the thermal conductivity falls. Lattice distortions and an improved phonon mean free path brought on by lattice thermal expansion at elevated temperatures are thought to be the causes of these events [42, 43]. 3.4 Mechanical properties Composition and microstructure have a significant impact on mechanical characteristics. While flaws are essential microstructural elements that substantially affect mechanical properties, the composition determines elastic characteristics and atomic interactions that determine dislocation behaviors [12, 44]. 3.4.1 Hardness Other factors should be considered to understand this wide range of hardness ratings. Whether the sample was produced utilizing liquid-state or solid-state processing will impact the manufacturing procedure [45, 46]. The hardness of an alloy depends on its constituent parts, how they interact with one another and other properties. Figure 6 depicts that most HEAs have harder surfaces than standard alloys [47]. Figure 6. Hardness extremes and minimums of the most researched HEA systems [11] 3.4.2 Tensile properties Tensile ductility typically increases when the temperature drops. The phrase “tensile ductility” can be used to characterize both the uniform plastic strain at the maximum load and the fracture-causing plastic strain, which also includes the uneven strain following necking. 3.4.3 Compressive properties Since HEAs have exceptional mechanical properties, compressive characteristics are frequently researched at room and high temperatures. According to Yeh (2013) [23], HEA has variable behavior depending on temperature and strain rate. During compression tests, HEAs suggest the existence of several deformation processes [11]. 129 3.4.4 Creep resistance In high-temperature applications, creep is crucial because it behaves in line with the potential law when the temperature is high enough to allow dislocation movement but the viscous law when it is low enough to prohibit it. Often, HEAs show significant creep resistance [46]. 3.4.5 Wear resistance Wear is a significant issue since HEAs exhibit greater competitiveness and the ability to be used in tools, molds, and structural components. Both sticky and abrasive environments have been examined to determine how HEAs wear [22]. The Co1.5CrFeNi1.5Ti and Al0.2Co1.5CrFeNi1.5Ti alloys, according to Chuang et al. [48] exhibit more wear resistance than normal wear-resistant steels of the same hardness. These HEAs’ extraordinary anti-oxidation and thermal softening resistance are thought to play a substantial role in their outstanding wear resistance. Alloying has the potential to affect the wear properties of HEAs [49]. 3.4.6 Fatigue behavior One of the most important variables that must be addressed and investigated in many prospective applications for HEAs is fatigue behavior and lifespan prediction, whether we are looking for use in the aerospace sector or other domains. In their investigation of the Al0.5CoCrCuFeNi HEA’s fatigue behavior, Hemphill et al. [50] compared the results to those of other popular alloys, such as titanium alloys, advanced BMGs, and steels. Compared to steel, titanium, and nickel alloys, the bottom limit of the HEA fatigue ratios performs better than several Zr-based BMGs and zirconium alloys. Moreover, due to their brittle nature, some materials, such as wrought aluminum alloys and ultra-high-strength steels, have lower fatigue ratios despite having higher tensile strengths. 3.4.7 Corrosion resistance According to Zhang et al. [22] and Heydari et al. [51], HEAs have excellent corrosion resistance. For starters, high entropy alloys have better corrosion resistance than several commonly used stainless steel types because their crystal structures tend to be simpler. Another factor is that many high entropy alloys have dendritic segregation structures, comprising a basic body structure and surface structure. Dendritic segregation structures also contain multiple disordered structures and nanoparticles. Introducing these structures has considerably increased the corrosion resistance of high entropy alloys. For instance, the considerably increased CoCrFeSi displayed improved corrosion resistance compared with stainless steel 304 in 1.0 mol/L NaCl and 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 solutions at ambient temperature [19]. 3.4.8 Density For many applications, alloy density is a crucial factor to consider. The densities of conventional alloys are frequently grouped according to the weights of the constituent elements: 2.7 g/cm3 for aluminum alloys, 4.5 g/cm3 for titanium alloys, 7.9 g/cm3 for steels, and 8 to 9 g/cm3 for superalloys based on nickel. Density is more equally distributed for CCAs. Light metal CCAs range in density from (2.67-5.21) g/cm3, while refractory CCAs range from 5.59 to 13.75 g/cm3. For MPEAs made of 3D transition metals, densities aren’t typically measured. They normally range between 5.1 and 8.9 grams per cubic centimeter, under a weighted mixing rule. As a result, HEAs have favorable corrosion, electrochemical, wear-resistance, and oxidation behavior features [12, 52]. 3.5 Functional properties HEAs are recently created materials for which the study has only focused on the mechanical and microstructural aspects. On the functional qualities of the HEAs, some improvement is seen. These attributes include soft-magnetic properties, radiation resistance, catalytic properties, and thermoelectric qualities. These traits are influenced by their unique multi-principal element solid solution structure. The functional features are crucial for applications such as electromagnetic interference shielding, nuclear magnetic resonance, and solar plates. Examples of materials that help store hydrogen duels include CoFeMnTiVZr, TiZrNbMoV, HfNbTiVZr, and ZrTiVCrFeNi because they provide greater bulk density, increased safety, and reversibility in gas storage and sensing applications [14]. Soft magnetic materials [53-55] irradiation resistance materials [56-59], superconducting materials [60], photothermal conversion materials [61, 62], diffusion barrier films [63, 64] are only a few of the functional areas where HEAs have made significant strides. 4. OPERATING AT SEVERE WORKING EVENTS In alloy engineering, the ideal alloy has a low density and high strength/ hardness [65]. In construction purposes like civil transportation and aeronautical engineering, lowering the weight of engineering elements is essential for reducing energy demand. Without HEAs, an aircraft corrodes, oxidizes, and cannot withstand extremely considerable temperatures [66]. Under typical operating conditions, power plants without HEAs as fuel cladding materials exhibit low thermal conductivity, a high thermal neutron absorption cross-section, and corrosion [67, 68]. An air foil bearing is an example of a part used at high temperatures but not requiring liquid lubrication. The system, which holds up the rotating shaft, comprises a top and a bump foil. The top foil and the journal, which are in touch with the shaft when it is stationary, are separated by a thin layer or gap when moving at higher speeds because of hydrodynamic pressure [69]. As a result, the bearing strikes the race at a slow rate during startup acceleration at standard temperature and shutdown deceleration at high temperature. These working circumstances are unpredictable. Thus, the materials must provide lubricity throughout a wide temperature range and long duration. A gas turbine engine’s rolling element bearing and the bearing and piston of an automobile diesel engine all function at high temperatures similarly to air foil bearings [70]. Similar to gas turbine engines to work efficiently (requiring higher temperatures, faster speeds, and higher contact pressures), the design and operating circumstances of mechanical systems must be drastically revised [71-74]. 5. A prospective future goal for HEAs is to design and build potential special applications to satisfy new requirements as opposed to merely replicating current performance [75]. # High Entropy Alloy Powder vs. Traditional Alloys: A Comparative Analysis

# High Entropy Alloy Powder vs. Traditional Alloys: A Comparative Analysis

Materials are the backbone of technology and industry, and the quest for new and improved materials is a driving force behind innovation. High entropy alloys (HEAs) have emerged as a remarkable class of materials, offering unique properties and performance when compared to traditional alloys. In this article, we will conduct a comparative analysis between high entropy alloy powder and traditional alloys, exploring the key differences and advantages that HEAs bring to the table.

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1695287138-normal-high-entropy-alloys-powder.jpg)

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1695287138-normal-high-entropy-alloys-powder.jpg)

## **Understanding High Entropy Alloys**

[High entropy alloys](https://samaterials.com/high-entropy-alloys-for-thermoelectric-applications.html), often referred to as multi-principal element alloys, are a relatively new class of materials that deviate from the conventional alloy design philosophy. Instead of relying on a single dominant element, HEAs are composed of multiple elements, typically five or more, in nearly equiatomic proportions. This unique composition results in a complex microstructure that gives rise to exceptional properties.

## **Composition and Structure: HEAs vs. Traditional Alloys**

**Diversity of Elements:** Traditional alloys often consist of a primary metal alloyed with one or two additional elements. In contrast, HEAs incorporate a wide range of elements, creating a "high entropy" of atomic configurations.

**Complex Microstructure:** The equiatomic distribution of elements in HEAs leads to a complex, disordered crystal structure, which can enhance mechanical properties.

**Customizability:** Traditional alloys are limited by the properties of their base metals, whereas HEAs offer greater flexibility in tailoring properties to specific needs.

## **Mechanical Properties**

**Strength:** HEAs often exhibit exceptional strength, making them suitable for high-stress applications. Traditional alloys may require extensive alloying or heat treatment to achieve comparable strength.

**Ductility:** Some traditional alloys excel in ductility, but HEAs can offer a balance between strength and ductility that is difficult to achieve in traditional materials.

**Hardness:** HEAs can be harder than traditional alloys, providing enhanced wear resistance in certain applications.

## **Thermal Stability**

**High-Temperature Resistance:** HEAs are known for their remarkable stability at high temperatures, making them suitable for applications where traditional alloys may fail.

**Oxidation Resistance:** Some HEAs demonstrate excellent resistance to oxidation and corrosion, surpassing the performance of traditional alloys.

## **Weight and Density**

**Lightweight:** HEAs can be designed to have low density, making them ideal for lightweight structural components, whereas traditional heavy alloys may not be as suitable.

## **Applications**

**Aerospace:** HEAs show promise in aerospace applications, where their high strength, lightweight, and high-temperature stability are advantageous.

**Energy:** Traditional alloys are used in energy applications, but HEAs are being explored for advanced energy storage and generation systems.

**Medical:** HEAs are emerging as potential materials for biomedical implants due to their biocompatibility and mechanical properties.

## **Challenges**

**Processing Complexity:** HEAs can be challenging to manufacture due to their complex compositions and microstructures.

**Cost:** Traditional alloys benefit from established manufacturing processes, making them cost-effective, while HEAs may require more research and development to become cost-competitive.

## **Conclusion**

[High entropy alloy powder](https://powder.samaterials.com/high-entropy-alloy-powder.html) represents a paradigm shift in materials science. Their unique composition, complex microstructure, and exceptional properties set them apart from traditional alloys. While HEAs hold great promise in various industries, they are not a one-size-fits-all solution. The choice between HEAs and traditional alloys depends on the specific requirements of each application, considering factors like mechanical properties, thermal stability, weight, and cost.

As research in high entropy alloys continues to advance, we can expect to see an increasing number of applications where the unique properties of HEAs provide significant advantages over traditional alloys, driving innovation and pushing the boundaries of what is possible in materials science and engineering.

enriched_points.expanded_content: High-entropy alloys (HEAs) outperform traditional alloys through superior strength, thermal stability, and corrosion resistance, achieved via complex microstructures and diverse elemental compositions.

HEAs are suitable for aerospace, biomedical, and energy sectors, offering unique properties for high-stress components, medical implants, and efficient energy systems, thus expanding their industrial relevance.
Ongoing research into HEAs focuses on optimizing their properties and manufacturing processes, crucial for advancing material science and meeting the demands of Industry 4.0.
